title: Anesthesia for ENT Surgery MCQ – Chapter 34
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: Why is ENT anesthesia considered one of the most difficult?

options.C: Because airway is often difficult, surgeon and anesthetist share the common field, and complications are related to airway

questions.explanation: ENT anesthesia is one of the most difficult because quite often airway is difficult, surgeon and anesthetist share the common field and complications are related to airway.

questions.question: What does panendoscopy involve?

options.B: Laryngoscopy, bronchoscopy, and esophagoscopy (also called triple endoscopy)

questions.explanation: Panendoscopy involves laryngoscopy, bronchoscopy, and esophagoscopy. It is also called as triple endoscopy.

questions.question: What happens to the glottic opening due to laryngeal growth in panendoscopy patients?

options.B: It may be reduced to the extent that even small tube insertion becomes impossible

questions.explanation: Due to the growth, the glottic opening may be reduced and sometimes to an extent that even insertion of the small size tube becomes impossible.

questions.question: What is the incidence of myocardial ischemia and arrhythmias due to laryngeal manipulation?
questions.explanation: Laryngeal manipulation causes sympathetic stimulation. Incidence of myocardial ischemia and arrhythmias is significant (2-4%).

questions.question: What must be done during preoperative evaluation for panendoscopy patients?

options.B: Assessment of airway by preoperative endoscopy or indirect laryngoscopy is must

questions.explanation: During preoperative evaluation, assessment of airway by preoperative endoscopy or indirect laryngoscopy is must.

questions.question: Why are IV lignocaine, IV fentanyl and topical anesthesia of the larynx important for panendoscopy?
questions.explanation: Intravenous (IV) lignocaine, IV fentanyl and topical anesthesia of the larynx are very important to prevent laryngeal reflexes.

questions.question: What are MLS tubes?

options.D: Pediatric size tubes with cuff available in 5, 5.5 and 6 mm size

questions.explanation: Microlaryngeal surgery (MLS) tubes are pediatric size tubes with cuff. They are available in 5, 5.5 and 6 mm size.

questions.question: In what percentage of patients is it possible to get surgery done with MLS tube?
questions.explanation: In the majority (>95%) of the patients it is possible to get the surgery done with MLS tube.

questions.question: What is the disadvantage of MLS tubes?

options.A: High ventilatory pressures are required to overcome the narrow size of tube
options.C: They cannot be used in adults

questions.explanation: The disadvantage is that high ventilatory pressures are required to overcome the narrow size of tube.

questions.question: In High Frequency Positive Pressure Ventilation (HFPPV), what is the frequency of breaths delivered?
questions.explanation: In HFPPV, small tidal volumes are delivered at high frequency (60-100 breaths/min) at a high pressure (60 psi) through a stiff 3.5-4.0 mm catheter passed through the vocal cord.

questions.question: What is the diameter of the catheter used in HFPPV?
questions.explanation: A stiff 3.5-4.0 mm catheter is passed through the vocal cord for HFPPV.

questions.question: In jet ventilation, where is the tip of the jet kept?

options.A: Within the laryngoscope to avoid direct barotrauma to trachea

questions.explanation: The tip of the jet is kept within the laryngoscope to avoid direct barotrauma to the trachea and gas is delivered at high pressure (50-60 psi).

questions.question: What is the apnea technique in ventilation for panendoscopy?

options.C: Intubation is done, adequate ventilation given, tube removed for 1-2 minutes for surgery, then reintubated if needed

questions.explanation: In apnea technique intubation is done and after adequate ventilation tube is removed for short period (1-2 minutes or till oxygen saturation starts falling). In this period surgeon performs the procedure.

questions.question: How is anesthesia maintained during panendoscopy?

options.A: With inhalational agent and repeated doses of succinylcholine for small procedures or nondepolarizing agent for prolonged surgery

questions.explanation: Anesthesia is maintained with inhalational agent and repeated doses of succinylcholine for small procedures or nondepolarizing agent for prolonged surgery.

questions.question: Why should nitrous oxide be avoided in laryngeal surgeries?

options.D: It can increase postsurgical edema by causing expansion of the cuff of endotracheal tube

questions.explanation: Nitrous oxide should be avoided because it can increase the postsurgical edema by causing expansion of the cuff of endotracheal tube.

questions.question: When is Heliox administration strongly considered in ENT?
questions.explanation: Administration of Heliox is strongly considered in patients with stridor.

questions.question: What is the most important complication of laser surgery?
questions.explanation: The most important complication of laser surgery is fires, therefore the most important aim should be to prevent fire.

questions.question: What FiO2 should be used during laser surgery to prevent fire?
questions.explanation: Use low FiO2 (<30%) to prevent fire during laser surgery.

questions.question: Why should N2O be avoided during laser surgery?
questions.explanation: Avoid N2O as it can support fire.

questions.question: What special kind of endotracheal tube is used for laser surgery?
questions.explanation: Use special kind of laser resistant endotracheal tube.

questions.question: What should the tube cuff be filled with during laser surgery instead of air?
questions.explanation: Fill the tube cuff with saline (instead of air) during laser surgery.

questions.question: What is the FIRST step if fire occurs during laser surgery?

options.C: Immediately remove tube and submerge in water

questions.explanation: If fire occurs: immediately remove tube and submerge in water.

questions.question: What is the second step in the fire protocol after removing the tube?

options.D: Immediately stop oxygen (and nitrous oxide) and start ventilation with air by mask

questions.explanation: Immediately stop oxygen (and nitrous oxide) and start ventilation of the patient with air by mask.

questions.question: What volume of cold saline should be used to flush the pharynx after airway fire?
questions.explanation: Flush pharynx with 50-60 mL of cold saline.

questions.question: When may tracheostomy be required after an airway fire?

options.D: If damage is severe enough not to allow intubation

questions.explanation: Tracheostomy may be required if damage is severe enough not to allow intubation.

questions.question: Under what type of anesthesia is flexible bronchoscopy for diagnostic biopsies usually performed?
questions.explanation: Flexible bronchoscopy, usually done for diagnostic biopsies, can be performed under topical anesthesia.

questions.question: What is rigid bronchoscopy usually done for?

options.A: Foreign body removal under general anesthesia

questions.explanation: Rigid bronchoscopy, usually done for foreign body removal is performed under general anesthesia.

questions.question: What are the ventilation methods for rigid bronchoscopy?

options.A: High frequency jet ventilation, insufflation, and apnea technique

questions.explanation: Ventilation during rigid bronchoscopy is achieved with: high frequency jet ventilation through the oxygen port, insufflation, and apnea technique.

questions.question: What is the majority of patients undergoing adenotonsillectomy?
questions.explanation: Majority of the patients for adenotonsillectomy/tonsillectomy are pediatric.

questions.question: If there are no adenoids, what route should be preferred for nasal intubation?

options.D: Nasal intubation should preferably be avoided due to increased chances of bleeding

questions.explanation: If there are no adenoids nasal intubation can be done, however due to increased chances of bleeding nasal intubation should preferably be avoided.

questions.question: What type of endotracheal tubes are most frequently used for tonsillectomy?
questions.explanation: Oral Ring-Adair-Elwyn (RAE) preformed tubes are most frequently used.

questions.question: Why should the throat be packed during tonsillectomy?

options.D: To keep the tube in place

questions.explanation: Throat should be packed to prevent aspiration.

questions.question: In what position should tonsillectomy patients be nursed postoperatively?

options.C: Tonsillar position - on one side (preferably left lateral) with head low

questions.explanation: These patients are nursed in tonsillar position, i.e. on one side (preferably left lateral) with head low to prevent aspiration and laryngospasm.

questions.question: How should a patient with post-tonsillectomy bleeding requiring re-exploration be managed?

options.A: As a full stomach patient (patient has swallowed blood causing significant aspiration risk)

questions.explanation: Such patient should be resuscitated and managed like full stomach patient considering that patient has swallowed enough blood which can cause significant aspiration.

questions.question: How should peritonsillar abscess and Ludwig angina ideally be drained?

options.B: Under local anesthesia (as intubation may be impossible due to trismus and aspiration risk)

questions.explanation: General anesthesia should be avoided as intubation at times may be impossible due to trismus and patient can aspirate the pus. Therefore, the abscesses should be drained under local anesthesia.

questions.question: If GA is required for peritonsillar abscess, what should be done before induction?

options.C: Awake nasal intubation with fibreoptic bronchoscopy

questions.explanation: If GA is required than awake nasal intubation with fibreoptic bronchoscopy should be achieved before induction of anesthesia.

questions.question: How many times more soluble is nitrous oxide compared to nitrogen?
questions.explanation: Nitrous oxide is 35 times more soluble than nitrogen.

questions.question: By how much can nitrous oxide increase middle ear pressure if Eustachian tube is blocked?

options.A: 375 mm H2O within half an hour

questions.explanation: If the Eustachian tube is blocked, nitrous oxide can increase the middle ear pressure to 375 mm H2O within half an hour.

questions.question: In which ear conditions should nitrous oxide NOT be used?

options.B: Patients with blocked Eustachian tubes (upper respiratory tract infection, adenoids, sinusitis and otitis media)

questions.explanation: Nitrous oxide should not be used in patients with blocked Eustachian tubes (upper respiratory tract infection, adenoids, sinusitis and otitis media).

questions.question: Why is nitrous oxide contraindicated for tympanoplasties?

options.A: Increase in middle ear pressure can displace the graft

questions.explanation: Increase middle ear pressure by nitrous oxide can displace the graft making nitrous oxide to be contraindicated for Tympanoplasties.

questions.question: When is increased middle ear pressure from N2O beneficial in ear surgery?

options.D: When surgeon is using 'inlay graft' (helps hold the graft)

questions.explanation: If the surgeon is using 'inlay graft' then increase in middle ear pressure is beneficial in holding the graft.

questions.question: What is very high in incidence after ear surgeries requiring prophylaxis?
questions.explanation: The incidence of postoperative nausea and vomiting is very high in ear surgeries; therefore prophylaxis with antiemetics is must.

questions.question: What is the Samter triad that must be ruled out in nasal surgery patients?

options.D: Nasal polyps, asthma, and sensitivity to aspirin/NSAIDs

questions.explanation: In the preoperative evaluation always rule out the Samter triad (nasal polyps, asthma, and sensitivity to aspirin/NSAIDs).

questions.question: Why is throat pack a must in nasal surgery?
questions.explanation: Throat pack is must to prevent aspiration of blood.

questions.question: Why must nasal surgery patients be counseled to breathe from mouth preoperatively?

options.C: Because they can become very restless in postoperative period after nasal packing if not trained

questions.explanation: The patients must be counseled and trained in the preoperative period to take breathing from mouth otherwise they can become very restless in the postoperative period after nasal packing.

questions.question: Why may muscle relaxants be deterred in parotid surgery?

options.D: Because continuous monitoring of facial nerve function is needed

questions.explanation: Continuous monitoring of facial nerve function may deter the anesthetist to use muscle relaxants.

questions.question: Why is obstructive sleep apnea surgery very challenging for anesthetists?

options.C: All concerns related to obese patients especially difficult airway management

questions.explanation: All concerns related to obese patients especially difficult airway management makes obstruction sleep apnea (OSA) surgeries (uvulopalatoplasties) to be very challenging to anesthetist.

questions.question: What does limited mouth opening in TMJ surgeries mandate?
questions.explanation: Due to the limited mouth opening intubation mandates awake nasal intubation.

questions.question: Are laser resistant endotracheal tubes 100% protective against laser fires?

options.A: No, they are not 100% protective against laser fires

questions.explanation: Laser resistant endotracheal tubes are not 100% protective against laser fires.

questions.question: What laryngeal complication can stimulation during panendoscopy induce?
questions.explanation: Laryngeal stimulation can induce laryngospasm.
